title: Deno Intro
content:
Deno Introduction

What is Deno?

Deno is a simple, modern, and secure runtime for JavaScript, TypeScript, and WebAssembly that uses V8 and is built in Rust.



  • Secure by Default: No file, network, or environment access, unless explicitly enabled.
  • TypeScript Support: Supports TypeScript out of the box without the need for manual transpilation.
  • Single Executable: Deno is delivered as a single executable file with no dependencies.
  • Built-in Utilities: Includes a set of built-in tools like a dependency inspector (deno info) and a code formatter (deno fmt).

title: Installation
content:
Installing Deno

Quick Start

Deno ships as a single executable with no dependencies. You can install it using the installers below.

Installation Commands
# Shell (Mac, Linux):
curl -fsSL https://deno.land/x/install/install.sh | sh

# PowerShell (Windows):
iwr https://deno.land/x/install/install.ps1 -useb | iex

# Homebrew (Mac):
brew install deno

# Chocolatey (Windows):
choco install deno

title: First Script
content:
Your First Deno Script

Hello World

Unlike Node.js, Deno can run scripts directly from URLs or local files without a package.json.

hello.ts
console.log("Welcome to Deno!");

Run it with:

deno run hello.ts

title: Security
content:
Deno Security

Secure by Default

By default, Deno scripts have no permissions to access the disk, network, or environment variables. You must explicitly grant permissions using flags.



  • --allow-read: Allow file system read access.
  • --allow-write: Allow file system write access.
  • --allow-net: Allow network access.
  • --allow-env: Allow environment access.
  • --allow-all or -A: Allow all permissions (not recommended for production).
Running with Permissions
deno run --allow-net='google.com' main.ts

title: Standard Library
content:
Standard Library

No NPM Needed

Deno provides a comprehensive standard library (std) that is reviewed by the Deno maintainers and guaranteed to work with a specific Deno version.

Importing from STD
import { copy } from "https://deno.land/std@0.224.0/io/copy.ts";
import { assert } from "https://deno.land/std@0.224.0/assert/mod.ts";

assert(true);

title: Fetch API
content:
Web APIs

Built-in Fetch

Deno implements standard Web APIs like fetch, so you don't need external libraries like axios or node-fetch.

API Call Example
const res = await fetch("https://api.github.com/users/denoland");
const json = await res.json();
console.log(json.name);

Note: This requires the --allow-net flag.

title: File System
content:
File System Operations

Reading and Writing

Deno provides modern, promise-based APIs for file system operations through the Deno namespace.

File I/O Example
const text = "Hello Deno!";
await Deno.writeTextFile("./hello.txt", text);

const content = await Deno.readTextFile("./hello.txt");
console.log(content);

title: HTTP Server
content:
Building a Web Server

Embedded Server

Creating an HTTP server in Deno is incredibly simple with the Deno.serve function.

server.ts
Deno.serve((_req) => {
  return new Response("Hello, World!");
});

Run with: deno run --allow-net server.ts

title: Testing
content:
Testing in Deno

Built-in Test Runner

Deno has a built-in test runner for both JavaScript and TypeScript code, so you don't need Jest or Mocha.

my_test.ts
import { assertEquals } from "https://deno.land/std/assert/mod.ts";

Deno.test("url test", () => {
  const url = new URL("https://deno.land");
  assertEquals(url.href, "https://deno.land/");
});

Run tests with: deno test

title: Deno vs Node
content:
Deno vs Node.js

Key Differences

Created by Ryan Dahl (who also created Node.js), Deno aims to fix many of the "mistakes" in Node's original design.



  • Security: Node allows full access to all resources; Deno allows zero by default.
  • Modules: Node uses npm and node_modules; Deno uses URLs and caches dependencies.
  • TypeScript: Node requires a compiler; Deno has one built-in.
  • Package Manager: Node needs npm; Deno doesn't require a package manager.

title: Deno Deploy
content:
Zero Config Deployment

Deploying to the Edge

Deno Deploy is a distributed system that runs JavaScript, TypeScript, and WebAssembly at the edge, worldwide.



  • Fast: Scripts run close to users in 35+ regions.
  • Simple: Just connect your GitHub repo and push code.
  • Native: Uses the exact same Deno runtime you use locally.

title: Deno KV
content:
Deno KV

Built-in Key-Value Store

Deno KV is a zero-config, ACID-compliant key-value database built directly into the Deno runtime.

KV Example
const kv = await Deno.openKv();

await kv.set(["users", "alice"], { name: "Alice", age: 30 });

const entry = await kv.get(["users", "alice"]);
console.log(entry.value);

title: Deno Queues
content:
Deno Queues

Asynchronous Messaging

Deno Queues allow you to offload heavy tasks to the background or communicate between different parts of your application asynchronously.

Queue Example
const kv = await Deno.openKv();

// Listen for messages
kv.listenQueue((msg) => {
  console.log("Received message:", msg);
});

// Send a message
await kv.enqueue({ hello: "world" });

title: Deno Cron
content:
Deno Cron

Scheduled Tasks

Deno Cron allows you to create scheduled tasks with a simple, familiar syntax, without needing external tools like `cron` or `pm2`.

Cron Example
Deno.cron("Sample Task", "*/15 * * * *", () => {
  console.log("This task runs every 15 minutes");
});

title: Deno Jupyter
content:
Deno Jupyter

Interactive Notebooks

Deno has native support for Jupyter notebooks, allowing you to run JavaScript and TypeScript interactively in environments like VS Code or Google Colab.

Installation
# Install the Deno kernel
deno jupyter --install

Once installed, you can create `.ipynb` files and select Deno as the runtime kernel.

title: Deno Compiling
content:
Deno Compile

Standalone Executables

Deno can compile your script and all its dependencies into a single, standalone executable that can run on systems without Deno installed.

Compilation Command
# Compile a script into a binary
deno compile --allow-read --allow-net server.ts

title: Deno Summary
content:
Deno Summary 🦕

You're Ready for Modern JS!

Congratulations! You've learned the fundamentals of Deno, the secure and modern JavaScript runtime.

  • Secure Foundations: You know how to manage permissions effectively.
  • Modern Tools: You've seen how build-in TS support and standard libraries simplify development.
  • Edge Ready: You understand how to build and deploy modern web applications.

Next Steps: Try building a REST API with Oak, explore the Deno Standard Library, or deploy your first app to Deno Deploy!
